violations.claim: What if proven and violations are both empty arrays in the loaded contract?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P6 - Boundary Analysis  
; What if proven and violations are both empty arrays in the loaded contract?
; The backward compat code would create an empty clause_history

(declare-const proven_count Int)
(declare-const violations_count Int)
(declare-const clause_history_count Int)

; Loaded contract has empty arrays
(assert (= proven_count 0))
(assert (= violations_count 0))

; Backward compat creates: [...proven.map(), ...violations.map()]
; Both maps on empty arrays produce empty results
(assert (= clause_history_count (+ proven_count violations_count)))

; Result: clause_history = [] for contracts that previously had no clauses
; Is this semantically valid? A contract with empty clause_history after loading
(assert (= clause_history_count 0))
(check-sat)
; sat → Empty contract can have empty clause_history after backward compat
; This may mask that the original contract had no properties to track

violations.claim: The backward compat creates history entries with all witness counts at 0
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5 - Semantic Correctness
; The backward compat creates history entries with all witness counts at 0
; This resets the weakening state - is this semantically correct?

(declare-const original_weaken_step Int)
(declare-const new_weaken_step Int)
(declare-const original_witness_count Int)
(declare-const new_witness_count Int)

; Older contract file: no clause_history exists
; But the contract MAY have had prior weakening in a previous session
(assert (> original_weaken_step 0))
(assert (> original_witness_count 0))

; Backward compat creates NEW clause_history with fresh state
(assert (= new_weaken_step 0))
(assert (= new_witness_count 0))

; Can prior weakening state be lost during migration?
(assert (> original_weaken_step new_weaken_step))
(check-sat)
; sat → Backward compat discards prior weakening state, resetting to 0
; Semantically: previously weakened clauses appear as fresh "active" clauses

violations.claim: loadFromDisk calls walk() which uses readdirSync - no preconditions
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P1 - Precondition Propagation
; loadFromDisk calls walk() which uses readdirSync - no preconditions
; The function propagates data from disk without validation
; What guarantees does the code establish about loaded contract validity?

(declare-const contract_file_hash String)
(declare-const contract_function String)
(declare-const contract_line Int)
(declare-const contract_signal_hash String)

; The loaded contract must have required fields per Contract interface
; But loadFromDisk does NOT validate these fields exist
(assert (= contract_file_hash ""))
(assert (= contract_function ""))
(assert (= contract_line 0))
(assert (= contract_signal_hash ""))

; After loading, these empty values can exist in this.contracts
; Subsequent code (recordWitness, weakenClause) expects valid identifiers
(assert (or (= contract_file_hash "") (= contract_function "")))
(check-sat)
; sat → Empty contract fields can be loaded from corrupted/malformed files
; No validation occurs on required Contract fields during loadFromDisk

(declare-const contract_file_hash String)

proven.claim: After loadFromDisk completes, this.contracts contains only successful loads
proven.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5 - Semantic Correctness
; After loadFromDisk completes, this.contracts contains only successful loads
; But the caller has NO way to know ANY files were skipped

(declare-const expected_files Int)
(declare-const loaded_files Int)
(declare-const skipped_files Int)
(declare-const total_contracts Int)

; Walk finds multiple JSON files in contracts directory
(assert (> expected_files 1))

; Some files parse successfully, some fail
(assert (= loaded_files 1))
(assert (= skipped_files (- expected_files loaded_files)))
(assert (> skipped_files 0))

; Only successfully parsed contracts are in this.contracts
(assert (= total_contracts loaded_files))

; The ContractStore has no way to report what was lost
(assert (= skipped_files 0))
(check-sat)
; sat → Caller cannot detect that contract files were skipped
; Missing contracts may cause downstream verification failures

violations.claim: What happens when JSON parses but contains malformed contract data?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P6 - Boundary and Degenerate Input Analysis
; What happens when JSON parses but contains malformed contract data?
; The catch block catches ANY error, but what specific corruption is skipped?

(declare-const json_valid Bool)
(declare-const contracts_count Int)
(declare-const valid_contracts Int)
(declare-const corrupted_contracts Int)

; Code path: JSON.parse succeeds but contracts array has issues
(assert json_valid)

; The loaded data has some valid and some corrupted contracts
(assert (> contracts_count 1))
(assert (= valid_contracts 1))
(assert (= corrupted_contracts (- contracts_count valid_contracts)))

; Loop processes ALL contracts in data.contracts
; If any contract causes an error during processing, ALL are lost
(assert (> corrupted_contracts 0))

; The catch block skips the ENTIRE file if any error occurs
; This is a boundary case: partial data loss due to any corruption
(check-sat)
; sat → Corrupted contracts cause entire file to be skipped
; Even one bad contract in a valid JSON file loses ALL contracts

violations.claim: Multiple files are processed in the loop (line 77)
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P2 - State Mutation Analysis  
; Multiple files are processed in the loop (line 77)
; If one file errors, it doesn't affect loading of other files

(declare-const file1_loads Bool)
(declare-const file2_loads Bool)
(declare-const file1_error Bool)
(declare-const file2_error Bool)

; First file in loop has error
(assert file1_error)
(assert (not file1_loads))

; Second file in loop succeeds
(assert (not file2_error))
(assert file2_loads)

; Loop continues after catch - file2 processing is independent
; Each iteration's try/catch is independent
(assert (and file2_loads (not file2_error)))

; Property: One file's corruption doesn't corrupt the entire store
(check-sat)
; sat → One bad file can be skipped while others load successfully
; The loop continues processing remaining files after catch

proven.claim: PROVEN PROPERTY: P3 - The function DOES correctly handle the documented transformations
proven.smt2:
; PROVEN PROPERTY: P3 - The function DOES correctly handle the documented transformations
; For any string input, it produces output with:
; 1. Comments removed (pattern ;[^\n]*)
; 2. Whitespace normalized (\s+ -> single space)
; 3. Leading/trailing whitespace trimmed

(declare-const input_string String)
(declare-const output_has_comments Bool)
(declare-const output_has_extra_whitespace Bool)
(declare-const output_has_leading_space Bool)
(declare-const output_has_trailing_space Bool)

; The function applies three transformations in sequence:
; Step 1: .replace(/;[^\n]*/g, "") removes all comment content
; After this, output_has_comments should be false
(assert (= output_has_comments false))

; Step 2: .replace(/\s+/g, " ") normalizes all whitespace to single spaces
; After this, output_has_extra_whitespace should be false  
(assert (= output_has_extra_whitespace false))

; Step 3: .trim() removes leading/trailing whitespace
; After this, no leading/trailing spaces
(assert (= output_has_leading_space false))
(assert (= output_has_trailing_space false))

; Negation: prove that any of these transformations can fail
(assert (or output_has_comments output_has_extra_whitespace output_has_leading_space))
(check-sat)
; unsat → The three documented transformations are correctly applied
; Comments removed, whitespace normalized, trim applied

violations.claim: What happens with various edge case inputs?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P6 - Boundary and Degenerate Input Analysis
; What happens with various edge case inputs?

(declare-const input_empty Bool)
(declare-const input_only_newlines Bool)
(declare-const input_only_comments Bool)
(declare-const normalized_output String)

; Case 1: Empty string input
(assert input_empty)
; Code: "".replace(/;[^\n]*/g, "").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim()
; Result: "" (empty string)

; Case 2: Only newlines
(assert input_only_newlines)
; Code: "\n\n\n".replace(/;[^\n]*/g, "").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim()
; Result: "" (all whitespace removed by trim)

; Case 3: Only comments (semicolons)
(assert input_only_comments)
; Code: "; comment".replace(/;[^\n]*/g, "").replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim()  
; Result: "" (comments removed, no content remains)

; Are these degenerate cases semantically valid?
; A clause that becomes "" after normalization - is this correct?
(assert (and input_only_comments (= normalized_output "")))
(check-sat)
; sat → Clauses that are only comments become empty string
; Empty normalized clause could match incorrectly in recordWitness

violations.claim: The function claims to produce "canonical form" - does it actually?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5 - Semantic Correctness
; The function claims to produce "canonical form" - does it actually?

(declare-const clause_a String)
(declare-const clause_b String)
(declare-const normalized_a String)
(declare-const normalized_b String)

; Two syntactically different but semantically equivalent SMT-LIB clauses
; Input A: has extra spaces around operators
; Input B: has minimal spacing

; The function normalizes whitespace but does NOT handle:
; - Commutative operators (e.g., (= x y) vs (= y x))
; - Redundant parentheses
; - Equivalent expressions

; Example: Are these considered equivalent?
(assert (= normalized_a normalized_b))

; The function only does: comment removal + whitespace normalization + trim
; It does NOT perform semantic normalization

; If the claim is "canonical form regardless of input variation"
; Then we should be able to prove two equivalent inputs produce same output

; But normalizeClause doesn't handle semantic equivalence
(check-sat)
; sat → The function produces syntactic normalization only
; Semantic equivalence (e.g., commutativity) is NOT handled
; The "canonical form" promise is not fully honored

violations.claim: normalizeClause doesn't mutate state - it's a pure transformation
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P7 - Arithmetic Safety (not applicable here - no arithmetic)
; PRINCIPLE: P2 - State Mutation Analysis
; normalizeClause doesn't mutate state - it's a pure transformation
; But it IS called multiple times in recordWitness

(declare-const clause1 String)
(declare-const clause2 String)
(declare-const norm1 String)
(declare-const norm2 String)

; recordWitness calls normalizeClause on the input clause (line 117)
; Then calls normalizeClause again on each h.clause in the loop (line 120)
; This is inefficient but not a bug

; Both calls use the same function - no state mutation concerns
(check-sat)
; sat → Multiple calls to normalizeClause are independent
; No state mutation to consider here

proven.claim: What if the clause exists but has never been weakened?
proven.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P3 - Calling Context Analysis
; What if the clause exists but has never been weakened? 
; The initial state from loadFromDisk has witness_count_at_last_weaken = 0

(declare-const current_witness_count Int)
(declare-const witness_count_at_last_weaken Int)
(declare-const can_strengthen Bool)

; The backward compat code initializes both to 0
(assert (= witness_count_at_last_weaken 0))
(assert (= current_witness_count 0))

; The function returns: current > last_weaken
; 0 > 0 = false
(assert (= can_strengthen (= current_witness_count witness_count_at_last_weaken)))

; But if a witness was recorded (current > 0) after load, can we strengthen?
(assert (= current_witness_count 1))
(assert (= witness_count_at_last_weaken 0))

; First evidence arrives after loading - can we strengthen?
(assert (not can_strengthen))
(check-sat)
; sat → A never-weakened clause that receives first witness returns false
; But technically new evidence HAS arrived - is this semantically correct?

; PROVEN PROPERTY: P4 - Temporal Analysis  
; When matching clause IS found, the comparison is temporally consistent

(declare-const current_count Int)
(declare-const last_weaken_count Int)
(declare-const result Bool)

; The function correctly compares the two values at the moment of call
(assert (= result (> current_count last_weaken_count)))

; If current > last, new evidence arrived since last weaken
; If current <= last, no new evidence

; This is the correct temporal state at call time
; recordWitness mutates current_witness_count (increments)
; weakenClause mutates witness_count_at_last_weaken (snapshots)
; canStrengthen reads both - sees the current state

; Negation: prove comparison is wrong when clause is found
(assert (not (= result (> current_count last_weaken_count))))
(check-sat)
; unsat → When clause is found, comparison correctly reflects state

violations.claim: Compare with weakenClause - does canStrengthen use the same matching logic?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P3 - Calling Context Analysis
; Compare with weakenClause - does canStrengthen use the same matching logic?

(declare-const clause_a String)
(declare-const clause_b String)
(declare-const can_str Bool)
(declare-const weaken_result Bool)

; weakenClause uses: h.clause === clause (exact match)
; canStrengthen uses: h.clause === clause (exact match)

; Both functions use exact string equality - no normalization!
; But recordWitness uses normalizeClause before comparison

; This creates an inconsistency:
; - recordWitness normalizes both input and stored clause
; - canStrengthen uses exact match without normalization
; - weakenClause uses exact match without normalization

; A clause passed to recordWitness might be normalized to match
; But the same clause passed to canStrengthen won't match if stored differently

(assert (= clause_a "(assert (> x 0))"))
(assert (= clause_b "(assert (> x 0))  "))

; Two semantically identical clauses, different whitespace
(assert (not (= clause_a clause_b)))

; canStrengthen with clause_a returns false (no match)
; But recordWitness with clause_a would increment (normalized match)
; canStrengthen with clause_b also returns false

; Inconsistency between recordWitness and canStrengthen matching
(check-sat)
; sat → canStrengthen uses exact match while recordWitness uses normalized match
; This can cause: recordWitness finds clause but canStrengthen returns false

violations.claim: What is the semantic meaning of "can strengthen" vs "clause not found"?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5 - Semantic Correctness
; What is the semantic meaning of "can strengthen" vs "clause not found"?

(declare-const contract_found Bool)
(declare-const clause_found Bool)
(declare-const return_value Bool)

; Scenario: contract at file:line exists, but clause doesn't exist
(assert contract_found)
(assert (not clause_found))

; Function returns false
(assert (= return_value false))

; False could mean:
; 1. Clause exists but no new evidence (true capability: cannot strengthen)
; 2. Clause doesn't exist (semantic error: clause not found)

; The caller cannot distinguish between these cases
; The return value doesn't accurately distinguish "cannot" vs "does not exist"

; The promise says "accurately reflect the capability"
; But it conflates two different meanings of false

(assert (and contract_found (not clause_found) (= return_value false)))
(check-sat)
; sat → False return value is ambiguous: no clause vs clause has no new evidence

violations.claim: Are there preconditions on the input parameters?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P1 - Precondition Propagation
; Are there preconditions on the input parameters?

(declare-const file_input String)
(declare-const line_input Int)
(declare-const clause_input String)

; Function searches: c.file === file && c.line === line && h.clause === clause
; No validation of inputs - any values work

; If caller passes non-existent file:line, returns false (correct - no contract)
; If caller passes valid file:line but wrong clause, returns false (correct - no match)

; No preconditions to establish - function handles any input gracefully
(check-sat)
; sat → No input validation needed - function handles all inputs safely

; PRINCIPLE: P3 - Calling Context Analysis

(declare-const clause_b String)
(declare-const can_str Bool)

; A clause passed to recordWitness might be normalized to match
; But the same clause passed to canStrengthen won't match if stored differently

(assert (= clause_a "(assert (> x 0))"))
(assert (= clause_b "(assert (> x 0))  "))

; Two semantically identical clauses, different whitespace
(assert (not (= clause_a clause_b)))

; canStrengthen with clause_a returns false (no match)
; But recordWitness with clause_a would increment (normalized match)
; canStrengthen with clause_b also returns false

; Inconsistency between recordWitness and canStrengthen matching
(check-sat)
; sat → canStrengthen uses exact match while recordWitness uses normalized match
; This can cause: recordWitness finds clause but canStrengthen returns false

; PRINCIPLE: P5 - Semantic Correctness
; What is the semantic meaning of "can strengthen" vs "clause not found"?

(declare-const contract_found Bool)
(declare-const clause_found Bool)
(declare-const return_value Bool)

; Scenario: contract at file:line exists, but clause doesn't exist
(assert contract_found)
(assert (not clause_found))

; Function returns false
(assert (= return_value false))

; False could mean:
; 1. Clause exists but no new evidence (true capability: cannot strengthen)
; 2. Clause doesn't exist (semantic error: clause not found)

; The caller cannot distinguish between these cases
; The return value doesn't accurately distinguish "cannot" vs "does not exist"

; The promise says "accurately reflect the capability"
; But it conflates two different meanings of false

(assert (and contract_found (not clause_found) (= return_value false)))
(check-sat)
; sat → False return value is ambiguous: no clause vs clause has no new evidence

; PRINCIPLE: P1 - Precondition Propagation
; Are there preconditions on the input parameters?

(declare-const file_input String)
(declare-const line_input Int)
(declare-const clause_input String)

; Function searches: c.file === file && c.line === line && h.clause === clause
; No validation of inputs - any values work

; If caller passes non-existent file:line, returns false (correct - no contract)
; If caller passes valid file:line but wrong clause, returns false (correct - no match)

; No preconditions to establish - function handles any input gracefully
(check-sat)
; sat → No input validation needed - function handles all inputs safely

violations.claim: What if multiple contracts share the same file:function:line key?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P2 - State Mutation Analysis
; What if multiple contracts share the same file:function:line key?

(declare-const contract1_hash String)
(declare-const contract2_hash String)
(declare-const duplicate_key String)
(declare-const map_value String)

; Two contracts at same file:function:line but with different content
(assert (not (= contract1_hash contract2_hash)))

; The Map stores: key -> hash
; When setting second contract, it OVERWRITES the first
; Only the LAST contract's hash remains in the Map

; But allCurrentHashes is built from Map.values()
; Which hash is in the set? The LAST one set (contract2_hash)

; If contract1 was a dependency of another contract, its hash is NOT in allCurrentHashes
; A contract that depends on contract1's hash would incorrectly be marked "not stale"

(assert (= map_value contract2_hash))
(assert (not (= map_value contract1_hash)))
(check-sat)
; sat → Duplicate keys in Map cause dependency hash mismatch
; A contract depending on earlier hash is incorrectly treated as not stale

violations.claim: What if a contract's depends_on list contains its OWN hash?
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5 - Semantic Correctness
; What if a contract's depends_on list contains its OWN hash?

(declare-const contract_self_hash String)
(declare-const depends_on_list String)
(declare-const in_current_hashes Bool)

; A contract might have itself as a dependency (circular)
; This can happen if withDependencies is called with same contract in context

(assert (= depends_on_list contract_self_hash))

; The check: depHash not in allCurrentHashes → stale
; Since contract's own hash IS in currentHashes (it was just computed)
; The dependency IS found → not marked stale

; Is this semantically correct? A contract depending on itself
; If the contract changed, should it be marked stale?
; The function says NO because the hash matches

(assert (= in_current_hashes true))
(check-sat)
; sat → Self-dependency always passes the check
; Contract doesn't detect its own changes as staleness

; PROVEN PROPERTY: P1 - Precondition Propagation
; The function correctly builds current hashes from all contracts

(declare-const num_contracts Int)
(declare-const map_size Int)
(declare-const key1 String)
(declare-const key2 String)

; For each contract, the key is computed and hash is stored
; If there are N contracts, Map should have N entries (or fewer with duplicates)

; The function builds currentHashes from ALL contracts in the array
(assert (= map_size num_contracts))

; This is the complete set of "current" hashes
; Any hash NOT in this set is considered "changed" (stale)

; Negation: prove some contract's hash is not in currentHashes
(assert (= map_size 0))
(check-sat)
; unsat → All contracts' hashes are included in currentHashes
